region_of_origin: China (Dim Sum tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class (FOODON_03302050); no specific yam-filled steamed bun entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers steamed bread and buns as a category; no specific yam-paste-filled variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for steamed yam bun. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One steamed yam bun (approximately 60 g), as typically served at a dim sum restaurant such as Din Tai Fung.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed bun with sweet filling — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA composite estimate for steamed wheat bun with taro paste filling
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for steamed buns
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for steamed yam-paste bun exists in any single approved source. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for steamed wheat bun dough (NDB 18033 and related entries), (2) FAO/INFOODS SEA taro/yam composition data, and (3) HPB Singapore data for similar steamed buns. The filling is approximately 40% taro paste by weight; the dough is approximately 60%. Energy and macros are calculated as a weighted composite. These values are representative of a standard 60 g steamed yam bun.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun (baozi) — analogous GI estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed white bread / steamed wheat products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed yam-paste bun. GI estimated at 65 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat buns and baozi-type products (GI range 55–68 reported for steamed wheat buns). The taro paste filling has a moderate GI (~55 per FAO/INFOODS and published literature on taro), which slightly moderates the overall GI relative to a plain steamed bun. A composite GI of 65 is used as a conservative medium-GI estimate. GL per serving = (65 × 26.4 g available carbohydrate per 60 g serving) / 100 = 17.2.

hypertension.guidance: Each steamed yam bun contains approximately 126 mg sodium per piece (60 g); individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one piece and avoid pairing with high-sodium dipping sauces or other salty dim sum dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate and sugar content with medium glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a medium GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of ~17 per piece, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to one piece per sitting, pair with protein or fibre-rich foods to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and account for the ~26 g of available carbohydrate per serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed yam bun is relatively low in saturated fat (~0.84 g per piece) and contains negligible cholesterol, making it an acceptable occasional choice for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, provided overall dietary sodium and refined carbohydrate intake are controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Yam Bun (芋泥包, Yù Ní Bāo) is a Chinese dim sum delicacy consisting of a soft, pillowy steamed wheat-flour bun encasing a smooth, lightly sweetened taro (yam) paste filling. The bun dough is made from wheat flour, water, sugar, lard or vegetable shortening, and a leavening agent, then hand-pleated into the characteristic 18-fold pattern associated with Din Tai Fung's craftsmanship before steaming. The taro filling is prepared from mashed premium taro (Colocasia esculenta or Dioscorea alata), blended with sugar and fat to achieve a creamy, subtly earthy, and mildly sweet flavour profile. The texture contrast between the fluffy, slightly chewy bun exterior and the dense, velvety filling is a hallmark of quality dim sum. Nutritionally, each piece (approximately 60 g) provides around 136 kcal, 26 g of carbohydrate, 2.9 g of protein, and 2.1 g of fat. The medium glycemic index (~65) and moderate glycemic load (~17) mean that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this item in moderation and pair it with lower-GI foods. Sodium content is moderate (~126 mg per piece), relevant for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian and dairy-free but contains gluten. It is commonly served as part of a dim sum spread in Cantonese and Taiwanese-style Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for steamed yam-paste bun exists in any single approved primary source. Values are estimated from a composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy steamed bun data, FAO/INFOODS SEA taro composition data, and HPB Singapore steamed bun reference values. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat buns. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component ingredients are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved source provides an exact match for steamed yam-paste bun. Values should be updated if an exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry becomes available. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source references used; taro contains small amounts of beta-carotene but a reliable per-100g figure for this specific prepared product is not available from approved sources. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food type. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food type. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food type. 6. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; confirmed negligible/zero for this steamed product as no hydrogenated fats are used in the standard recipe. 7. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0; confirmed negligible for a plant-based filled bun with no egg or dairy in the standard formulation. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0; naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard formulation. 9. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat buns; no direct measurement for steamed yam-paste bun was found. 10. Halal status is marked false as Din Tai Fung Singapore is not certified halal; individual consumers should verify. 11. Serving size of 60 g is an estimate based on typical dim sum bun weights; Din Tai Fung does not publicly disclose exact piece weights.
